did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a convolutional neural network (CNN) for pixel-wise classification and delineation of malignant basal cell carcinomas (BCCs), benign intradermal nevi (ID), and healthy skin using hyperspectral imaging data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper describes the development, calibration, and clinical pre-test of a novel SICSURFIS hyperspectral imager. It presents original experimental data, including the use of a convolutional neural network for pixel-wise classification of skin lesions (BCCs, ID, healthy skin) with specific accuracy metrics (0.89 and 0.72), and discusses system engineering and analysis aspects, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: FPI Based Hyperspectral Imager for the Complex Surfaces—Calibration, Illumination and Applications
brief_summary: This study introduces the SICSURFIS, a novel hand-held hyperspectral imager utilizing a piezo-actuated Fabry–Pérot interferometer and photometric stereo to capture spectral and 3D surface data from complex skin surfaces. The system was clinically pre-tested on 21 patients to differentiate malignant basal cell carcinomas (BCCs) from benign intradermal nevi (ID) and healthy skin. A convolutional neural network (CNN) was employed for pixel-wise classification using 31,168 augmented hyperspectral sub-cubes derived from histologically confirmed lesions. Evaluation via a slice-half model yielded an average accuracy of 0.89, while leave-one-out validation resulted in an average accuracy of 0.72 for typical lesions. The findings demonstrate that combining spectral data with surface models enables promising pixel-wise delineation of skin lesions, though further studies with larger datasets are needed to address generalization challenges.

lesion_types_studied.details: Malignant lesions studied in the classification task. 14 BCCs were imaged on 21 patients. Histologically confirmed. Included in the CNN pixel-wise classification with an average accuracy of 0.72 (leave-one-out) and 0.89 (slice-half).

Benign lesions studied in the classification task. 8 IDs were imaged on 21 patients. Histologically confirmed. Included in the CNN pixel-wise classification with an average accuracy of 0.72 (leave-one-out) and 0.89 (slice-half).
Non-lesion class used as a comparator in the pixel-wise classification task. Included in the CNN pixel-wise classification with an average accuracy of 0.72 (leave-one-out) and 0.89 (slice-half).

all_task_types.target: Pixel-wise classification of skin tissue into three discrete categories: malignant basal cell carcinomas (BCC), benign intradermal nevi (ID), and healthy skin.
all_task_types.details: The study employs a convolutional neural network (CNN) for pixel-wise classification using hyperspectral imaging data. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (BCC, ID, healthy skin) for each pixel. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (0.89 for slice-half model, 0.72 for leave-one-out on typical lesions), precision, recall/sensitivity, and F1-score. The task is explicitly described as 'pixel-wise classification' and 'delineation' of lesion types.

datasets_used.name: SICSURFIS Clinical Pre-Test Dataset
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset collected from 21 volunteering patients at Helsinki University Hospital during spring and autumn 2020. It consists of hyperspectral (HS), digital, and dermoscopic images of 14 malignant basal cell carcinomas (BCC) and 8 benign intradermal nevi (ID), along with healthy skin areas. The diagnoses were confirmed by dermatohistopathological analysis. The data was processed into surface albedo, normals, and 3D surface models using photometric stereo.

use.training: Used to train a convolutional neural network (CNN) for pixel-wise classification. The dataset was composed of 6,160 windowed HS-sub-cubes (30x30x34) from histologically confirmed lesions, which were augmented to 31,168 HS-sub-cubes. Training data was sourced from the left side of the lesions.
use.validation: Used for leave-one-out validation (17 models) and slice-half model validation to assess generalization and accuracy (average accuracy 0.72 for leave-one-out on typical lesions, 0.89 for slice-half).
use.testing: Used for testing the CNN models. Test data was sourced from the right side of the lesions (not used for training) in the slice-half approach, and left out entirely in the leave-one-out approach.
use.other: Used for system engineering, calibration, and clinical pre-test of the SICSURFIS hyperspectral imager.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the pixel-wise classification results and assess if the slice-half method caused biasing by training 17 models, each omitting one lesion's data.
To train the convolutional neural network using data from the left side of lesions and test on the right side, serving as a primary validation approach compared against leave-one-out.
To balance the training subset of windowed HS-sub-cubes.
To increase the size of the training data from 6,160 to 31,168 samples.
To evaluate and compare the performance of the slice-half model (0.89) and leave-one-out models (0.72 for typical lesions, 0.28 for unique/difficult lesions).
To report the numerical results of the pixel-wise slice half model’s test data (sensitivity 0.79, precision 0.81).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It stated a research hypothesis that surface models would improve performance, but evaluated this using descriptive accuracy metrics (average accuracy of 0.72 for leave-one-out and 0.89 for slice-half) and validation strategies rather than statistical significance testing.

slice_half_model.details: Average accuracy over the whole testing dataset for typical lesions.

leave_one_out_validation_typical_lesions.details: Average accuracy for 8 typical lesions without special features.

leave_one_out_validation_unique_difficul.details: Average accuracy for 9 lesions with unique features, of which 2 were unique and difficult.

leave_one_out_validation_all_samples.details: Average accuracy across all 17 leave-one-out models.

Lesion 5 (Unique).Unique Features: Broken skin, dark skin tone, convex surface

Lesion 2 (Unique).Unique Features: Wound or scab on top of lesion

Lesion 17 (Unique).Unique Features: The lesion and the healthy skin is covered by hair

Lesion 12 (Unique).Unique Features: Dark skin tone and red-brown lesion tone

Lesion 13 (Unique).Unique Features: Two types of lesions: naevus pigmentosus junctionalis on right side of the ID

Lesion 16 (Unique & difficult).Unique Features: Overall fair skin tone, small lesion (2mm), fair toned and uneven delineation

Lesion 6 (Unique & difficult).Unique Features: Lesion is pigmented, uneven skin tone around lesion

SICSURFIS Clinical Pre-Test Dataset.notes: The slice-half model achieved an average accuracy of 0.89. Leave-one-out validation showed an average accuracy of 0.72 for typical lesions and 0.28 for unique/difficult lesions. The CNN model distinguished malignant BCCs, benign IDs, and healthy skin at a pixel level. Visual classification maps indicated that reflections and skin color changes could cause misclassification.

architecture_details.spectral_branch: 3D Convolutional layers (3D conv11, 3D conv12) with LeakyReLU activation and max-pooling
architecture_details.depth_branch: 2D Convolutional layers (2D conv21, 2D conv22) with LeakyReLU activation and max-pooling
architecture_details.fusion: Flatten and concatenate outputs from 3D and 2D branches
architecture_details.hidden_layers: Fully connected layers: Dense 256, Dense 128, Dense 64, Dense 32, Dense 3
architecture_details.activation_functions: LeakyReLU used in convolutional and hidden layers
architecture_details.regularization: Dropout 0.5 applied after each dense layer

preprocessing_techniques: Dark reference acquisition (mean of 40 frames) and white reference acquisition (using white Teflon) for radiometric calibration.
Conversion of radiance images to reflectance images using white reference.
Combining visible (VIS) and very near-infrared (VNIR) hyperspectral images.
Calculation of albedo and surface normals using photometric stereo.
Calculation of depth maps from surface normals.
Bregman total variation denoising applied to albedo data in the spectral direction.
Smoothening of albedo and depth maps using scikit-image version 0.17.2.
Selection of the most significant depth frame (575 nm wavelength) to reduce dimensionality.
Setting infinity and non-numeral (NaN) values outside the field of view to 0.
Vertical slicing of images from the middle of each lesion to separate training (left side) and testing (right side) data.
Windowing of hyperspectral images into 30x30x34 HS-sub-cubes with a 5-pixel step.
Random selection of training pixels (250 lesion, 100 healthy skin) from the left side and testing pixels from the right side.
Creation of hand-drawn healthy skin masks based on ground truth images and RGB visualizations.
Data augmentation via vertical and horizontal flipping to increase training set size from 6,160 to 31,168 HS-sub-cubes.
Random over-sampling using the Imbalanced Learn library to balance the training subset.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training subset of windowed HS-sub-cubes to increase the dataset size from 6,160 to 31,168 samples. Test pixels were not flipped.
Used via the Imbalanced Learn library to balance the training subset of windowed HS-sub-cubes before augmentation.

conclusions: The novel SICSURFIS hyperspectral imager, combining a piezo-actuated Fabry–Pérot interferometer with photometric stereo, successfully captures spectral and 3D surface data from complex skin surfaces, enabling the delineation of malignant basal cell carcinomas (BCCs), benign intradermal nevi (ID), and healthy skin.

A convolutional neural network (CNN) utilizing hyperspectral data and surface models achieved promising pixel-wise classification performance, with an average accuracy of 0.89 in the slice-half model and 0.72 in leave-one-out validation for typical lesions.

The inclusion of 3D surface models alongside spectral data improved the machine learning model's performance, validating the hypothesis that surface information aids in clinical decision-making for complex topographies.

The study acknowledges significant limitations regarding generalization due to the small dataset size (21 patients, 14 BCCs, 8 IDs) and the presence of unique lesion features (e.g., hair, broken skin, dark skin tone) which caused significant drops in accuracy (average 0.28) for unique lesions in leave-one-out validation.

The slice-half validation method showed higher accuracy than leave-one-out validation, indicating potential bias in the slice-half approach; however, the correlation between the two methods suggests the model has generalization capabilities that require larger, unbiased datasets to fully realize.

Future work must address the need for larger datasets to reduce the impact of unique lesion features and cross-contamination bias, as well as technical improvements such as adopting MEMS FPI for device miniaturization and developing real-time FPGA-based solutions for clinical use.
important_remarks: The study is a clinical pre-test with a small sample size (21 patients, 14 BCCs, 8 IDs), limiting generalizability and statistical power.

The slice-half validation method (training on left side, testing on right side of the same lesion) likely introduces selection bias and inflates accuracy (0.89) compared to leave-one-out validation (0.72 for typical lesions).

Leave-one-out validation revealed significant performance drops (average accuracy 0.28) for lesions with unique features (e.g., hair, broken skin, dark skin tone), indicating poor generalization to atypical cases.
Data leakage risk exists in the slice-half approach as training and testing data come from the same lesion, sharing similar spectral and spatial characteristics.
The CNN model is sensitive to light reflections and uneven skin color changes, which cause misclassification of healthy skin as lesion pixels.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; results are reported as descriptive accuracy metrics.
The study acknowledges that the pixel-wise classification approach may overfit due to the limited number of unique lesions (17) despite data augmentation.
Future work requires larger, unbiased datasets and technical improvements (e.g., MEMS FPI) for clinical deployment.
